steps.title: Identify where the transitional phrase belongs
steps.content:
The sentence lists two pivotal events in the Latino rights movement: the founding of the League and the Katzenbach v.

Morgan decision. The phrase “for one” (meaning “for example”) should attach to the first event—the founding of the League—to mark it as one example in the series.

steps.title: Apply the rule for sentence boundaries with transitional phrases
steps.content:
A transitional phrase marking one item in a series should be attached to that item with a comma before it and a period after, creating a clear boundary before the next item begins as a new sentence.

Choice D (1929, for one. The) correctly places the comma after 1929, “for one,” then a period; this ensures “for one” modifies the founding and a new sentence introduces the Katzenbach decision with a capital “The.” Choice A (1929.

For one, the) places the period before “for one,” detaching it from the year and making it appear to introduce the second example instead.

Choice B (1929, for one, the) uses only commas, which fails to separate the two examples clearly.

Choice C omits the year entirely, removing the historical information.

That matches choice D — D is correct.
